summary: There are nine essential amino acids that cannot be synthesised by the body and must come from the diet.
There are twenty essential amino acids that must come from the diet.
All amino acids can be synthesised by the human body.
The four levels of protein structure are primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary.
The four levels of protein structure are primary, secondary, tertiary, and pentenary.
Protein structure has only two levels: primary and tertiary.
The urea cycle detoxifies ammonia in the liver, with carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I as the rate-limiting enzyme.
The urea cycle detoxifies ammonia in the kidney, with arginase as the rate-limiting enzyme.
The urea cycle occurs in the cytoplasm and is regulated by urease.
Phenylketonuria (PKU) is caused by phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency, leading to phenylalanine accumulation.
Phenylketonuria is caused by tyrosinase deficiency leading to tyrosine accumulation.
Phenylketonuria is an X-linked disorder affecting only males.
IgG is the most abundant immunoglobulin in serum and the only one that crosses the placenta.
IgM is the most abundant immunoglobulin in serum and crosses the placenta.
IgA is the most abundant immunoglobulin in serum.
IgA is the predominant immunoglobulin in mucosal secretions and breast milk.
IgE is the predominant immunoglobulin in mucosal secretions.
IgG is the predominant immunoglobulin in mucosal secretions.
IgE mediates type I hypersensitivity (allergic) reactions by binding to mast cells and basophils.
IgE mediates type IV delayed hypersensitivity reactions.
IgM mediates allergic reactions and anaphylaxis.
